figure_text: Fig. 8.2.  Two satellites A and B. Circles are shown on the surface of the Earth, the perimeters of which have equal signal arrival times from each of the satellites.

Fig. 8.3.  The projection of the circles from satellites A (orange) and B (blue) at two times T1 (solid) and T2 (dashed).  The signal at T1 alone fixes the receiver at either R1 or R1'. An additional satellite fix is required to remove the ambiguity. Alternatively, the signal at T2 can be used provided that the receiver is known not to have moved any appreciable distance. In choosing between R2 and R2', R2 makes better sense.
Fig. 8.4.  A solution on a plane for three satellites.  The three solid circles show constant arrival times for three satellites.  Two solutions are possible.

Figure 9.6. The Coanda effect blowing over a sheet of paper. (a) For flow in a straight line the pressures at A and B are the same. Flow over a curved surface will entrain stationary air from below creating curved streamlines. As we saw in Figure 2, this result in a region of low pressure which pulls the flow downwards. (b) the resulting flow follows the surface. With curved streamlines, the pressure at A is lower than at B, causing lift.

Figure 9.6. The Coanda effect blowing over a sheet of paper. (a) For flow in a straight line the pressures at A and B are the same. Flow over a curved surface will entrain stationary air from below creating curved streamlines. As we saw in Figure 2, this result in a region of low pressure which pulls the flow downwards. (b) the resulting flow follows the surface. With curved streamlines, the pressure at A is lower than at B, causing lift.

Figure 9.7. Examples of the Coanda effect. (c) Water being curved by a spoon. (d) The same effect is responsible for dribbly teapots.

Fig. 10.2. (a) A trolley is moving along a radial track on a rotating turntable. It's speed, v, measured relative to the table is constant but as it moves from P to P′ there is a sideways component of velocity due to the rotation of the table, which reduces with radius. (b) In time t, the trolley has moved from P to P′, but if the track exerted no sideways force on the trolley, it would end up P′′. The sideways force required is called the Coriolis force.

Fig. 10.6. Gyroscopic precession. (a) A spinning bicycle wheel supported on a string does not fall down, as expected intuitively, but precesses around a vertical axis (b) a side view of the bicycle wheel and its axle. The string pushes up with a force mg exactly equal to the weight of the wheel. The resulting moment mgd is responsible for the gyroscopic effect.

Fig. 10.6. Gyroscopic precession. (a) A spinning bicycle wheel supported on a string does not fall down, as expected intuitively, but precesses around a vertical axis (b) a side view of the bicycle wheel and its axle. The string pushes up with a force mg exactly equal to the weight of the wheel. The resulting moment mgd is responsible for the gyroscopic effect.
